coredata.dc:title: Fast electron transfer and enhanced visible light photocatalytic activity of silver and Ag2O co-doped titanium dioxide with the doping of electron mediator for removing gaseous toluene
coredata.dc:creator: Xue, Xiuling
Chen, Xiaoyi
Gong, Xiuwen
coredata.dc:description: Volatile organic compounds (VOCs) can influence air quality and threaten human health; however, the development of an effective method to remediate toxic VOCs is still remain challenge. Herein, a novel silver and Ag2O co-doped TiO2 with fast electron transfer modified by nitrogen doping carbon dots was prepared via a facile chemical precipitation-reduction method. Under 50 min of visible light irradiation, the novel photocatalyst exhibited approximately 50% gaseous toluene degradation efficiency, which was ca. 9.7 times and 2.2 times higher than that of bare TiO2 and silver and Ag2O co-doped TiO2, respectively. Importantly, the photocatalyst also showed a stable structure and good recycling ability after 400 min of photocatalytic degradation with little amount of NCDs. Besides, the up-conversion effect of NCDs could emit high-energetic light and thus generate more e−-h+ pairs, which could facilitate the SPR effect of Ag and thus showed strong visible light response because of the localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) excitation. Thus, the excellent photocatalytic performance could attribute to the synergetic effect of up-conversion effect of NCDs and SPR effect of silver which could both facilitate charge carriers separation and improve visible-light response. Finally, a possible mechanism for the excellent photocatalytic activity was proposed, in which the active species including ·O2 −, ·OH and h+ were generated and contributed to photocatalytic process. The successful fabrication of DACT provides a potential strategy for the treatment of VOCs in practical applications.

head.ce:title: Fast electron transfer and enhanced visible light photocatalytic activity of silver and Ag2O co-doped titanium dioxide with the doping of electron mediator for removing gaseous toluene

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering, Huaqiao University, Xiamen, 361021, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemical Engineering, Huaqiao University, Xiamen, 361021, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Volatile organic compounds (VOCs) can influence air quality and threaten human health; however, the development of an effective method to remediate toxic VOCs is still remain challenge. Herein, a novel silver and Ag2O co-doped TiO2 with fast electron transfer modified by nitrogen doping carbon dots was prepared via a facile chemical precipitation-reduction method. Under 50 min of visible light irradiation, the novel photocatalyst exhibited approximately 50% gaseous toluene degradation efficiency, which was ca. 9.7 times and 2.2 times higher than that of bare TiO2 and silver and Ag2O co-doped TiO2, respectively. Importantly, the photocatalyst also showed a stable structure and good recycling ability after 400 min of photocatalytic degradation with little amount of NCDs. Besides, the up-conversion effect of NCDs could emit high-energetic light and thus generate more e−-h+ pairs, which could facilitate the SPR effect of Ag and thus showed strong visible light response because of the localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) excitation. Thus, the excellent photocatalytic performance could attribute to the synergetic effect of up-conversion effect of NCDs and SPR effect of silver which could both facilitate charge carriers separation and improve visible-light response. Finally, a possible mechanism for the excellent photocatalytic activity was proposed, in which the active species including ·O2 −, ·OH and h+ were generated and contributed to photocatalytic process. The successful fabrication of DACT provides a potential strategy for the treatment of VOCs in practical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Air pollution has received growing attention from the whole world since it could cause negative impact on both the environment and human beings. Volatile organic compounds (VOCs) emitting from building materials, chemical manufacturing facilities, commercial products and etc. are the major pollutants that damage the environment and human beings [1–5]. As one of the VOCs, toluene is widely utilized as an organic solvent in adhesives and paints. Exposure to toluene could induce eye and throat irritation, sick building syndrome, memory impairment, central nervous system depression and even cause cancer [1-5]. Therefore, it is essential to design a novel method to effectively eliminate VOCs in the environment and thus improve the environment quality and decrease the negative effects on human health. At present, various methods, including chemical absorption, physical adsorption, microbial decomposition, photocatalysis, membrane filtration have been carried out to remove or degrade VOCs from the ambient environment [6,7]. Among them, semiconductor photocatalytic oxidation (PCO) technology has been regarded as the most efficient method due to high performance, low cost and mild operating conditions, which could convert the pollutants into CO2 and H2O without causing secondary pollution [3,7,8]. TiO2 has been widely used in the degradation of gaseous and liquid contaminants because of high chemical stability, biocompatibility, cheapness and excellent catalytic activity [9–11], pristine TiO2 still suffers from the fast recombination of photoinduced electron-hole (e−-h+) pairs and low visible-light utilization. Therefore, it is significant to develop a photocatalyst with visible light response property to overcome these problems of wide band gap energy materials [12].

Constructing a p-n heterojunction was an effective strategy to suppress the recombination of photoinduced e−-h+ pairs, which is very favorable for the rapid degradation of high concentration toluene. Ag2O could construct a p-n heterojunction with TiO2 due to their band gap structure, which could improve the light absorption and charge carriers separation [13]. However, Ag2O/TiO2 was unstable under visible light, which limited the practical application [14]. Silver (Ag) received researchers’ attention due to its appropriate work function and effective election injection. Many efforts have been reported in the use of Ag/TiO2 to enhance photocatalytic performance [15,16]. Therefore, Ag/Ag2O@TiO2 has attracted significant attention due to Ag-based materials could make better use of visible light [17-19] originated by SPR effect and Ag/Ag + system can effectively protect Ag2O from photocorrosion [20–24]. According to previous reports, the photocatalytic performance of Ag/Ag2O@TiO2 photocatalyst was higher than that of TiO2, which could be attributed to the outstanding properties of silver [25–28]. However, the photocatalytic performance of Ag/Ag2O@TiO2 is still barely satisfactory because the strength of SPR effect of Ag (around 425 nm) occurs only when free electrons are polymerized and oscillated on the catalyst surface, and the resonance is related to the wavelength of the incident light [29]. Therefore, due to the limitation of incident light intensity, the SPR effect cannot maximize the photocatalytic performance of the catalyst. According to previous work, the removal rate of toluene of Ag/Ag2O@TiO2 was less than 50% even under simulated sunlight irradiation [30]. Besides, there are still some drawbacks of Ag/Ag2O@TiO2: (1) most of the light sources used in the studies of other photocatalytic degradation of toluene on the continuous flow are ultraviolet light [31-33], which lead to the light utilization was far from enough because UV light accounts for only 8.7% of sunlight; (2) the amount of silver was around 10 at% generally, which increase the cost of fabrication; (3) the catalyst is difficult to be stable in the catalyst due to its photocorrosion, which is not benefit for the treatment of toluene on the continuous flow. Therefore, it is necessary to broaden light response range for improving photocatalytic performance. Recently, the carbon dots (CDs) with particle size less than 10 nm have attracted numerous interests owing to superior optical and up-conversion properties, outstanding chemical- and photo-stability, non-toxicity, and excellent electron-transfer properties [34–37]. Up to now, CDs have been introduced into the fabrication of photocatalysts such as CDs/TiO2 [38], CDs/C3N4 [39], CDs/ZnO [40], CDs/Ag3PO4 [41] and CDs/SiO2 [42]. Moreover, the further introduction of N atoms can also enhance the properties of CDs since N-doping could affect the electron structure of CDs and induce charge delocalication and thus lower the work function of the composite [43-46]. When irradiating by a certain intensity of light, the e− on the conduction band (CB) of the semiconductor will transfer to the N-doped CDs (NCDs), thus improving the separation of photoinduced e−-h+ pairs and suppressing the recombination of them. Wang et al. [47] synthesized different structures of TiO2 and modified it with NCDs to improve the photocatalytic degradation of diclofenac and tetracycline, the removal efficiency was 5.9-fold and 2.1-fold increase compared with modified TiO2. Yang et al. [48] fabricated a novel porous NCDs@g-C3N4 composite to remove methylene blue under visible light irradiation, demonstrating that NCDs could broaden the light response range and suppresses the photoinduced e−-h+ pairs recombination of g-C3N4. Based on the discussion above, the addition of NCDs could reduce the limitation of incident light and further broaden the optical response to the visible region and suppress the recombination of photoinduced charge carriers. Moreover, the up-conversion effect could facilitate the SPR effect and thus has a synergetic effect between NCDs and Ag, which may be a promising strategy to improve the photocatalytic performance of VOCs including gaseous toluene.

In this study, a novel double-Ag (silver and Ag2O) and nitrogen doping carbon dots (NCDs) co-doped titanium dioxide (DACT) photocatalyst was fabricated for the first time via a facile chemical precipitation-reduction method and was used for removing high concentration gaseous toluene. X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis showed that DACT photocatalyst was successfully fabricated. Furthermore, the optical properties of DACT photocatalyst was investigated by means of UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) and photoluminescence (PL) spectra. The main influencing parameters of photocatalytic degradation, such as the dosage of photocatalyst, inlet concentration and the velocity of gaseous toluene and relative humidity, were also investigated. Subsequently, a possible mechanism for the excellent photocatalytic activity was proposed.

All chemicals in this work were of analytical grade. Tetra-butyl titanate was from Aladdin Reagent CO., AgNO3 and toluene was purchased from Sinopharmaceutical Reagent CO. The experimental water was deionized (DI) water produced by a Milli-Q system.

NCDs was fabricated via a modified hydrothermal method [47,49,50]. In detail, 2.0 g of citric acid and 2.0 g of urea were dissolved in 15 mL of DI water and stirred for 10 min. The mixture was then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and kept at 180 °C for 6 h. After hydrothermal reaction, the solution was collected by centrifugation (12,000 rpm for 20 min). The supernatant then dried at 60 °C overnight. The resultant was re-dispersed in DI water with the concentration about 50 mg/mL and kept at 4 °C for further use.

TiO2 nanosheets were prepared by two-step synthetic method. In brief, 3.4 mL of hydrofluoric acid (40 wt%) were added into 25 mL of tetrabutyltitanate (TBT), followed by the addition of 30 mL of hydrogen peroxide solution (30 wt%) under stirring until forming an orange gel. During the hydrothermal reaction, the resultant suspension was then transferred to a 200 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 16 h. After solvothermal reaction, the obtained product was washed with DI water and ethanol for several times and then dried at 60 °C overnight. The obtained TiO2 was further calcined at 400 °C for 4 h to remove the residual F− remained on the surface.

The DACT was fabricated through a reduction-coprecipitation method. Typically, 1.0 g of NCDs@TiO2 (SI) was dissolved in 30 mL of DI water and stirred for 2 h and 0.5 M NaOH was added to adjust pH to 8.5. After that, 0.14 mL of AgNO3 solution (0.99 mM was added and stirred for 2 h. Then 1.4 mL of NaBH4 (0.25 mM) was added to the suspension drop by drop and setting for 16 h. The final product was centrifuged and washed with DI water and ethanol for several times, respectively, and then freeze-drying overnight. The amount of Ag in the product was 4 at%. Furthermore, DAT was also fabricated via the same process which using TiO2 instead of NCDs@TiO2.

Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured with an Edinburgh FL/FS900 spectrophotometer with an excitation wavelength of 320 nm to study the fluorescence properties and the charge separation capacity. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured with an X-ray diffractometer (Ultima Ⅳ 2036E102, Rigaku Corporation, Japan) to test the crystal phase of the composites. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were tested using Bruker FTIR for distinguishing chemical bonds and metal valence state. The microstructure and morphology of as-prepared samples were carried out by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Tescan mira3 Zeiss Merlin Compact) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100F). UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) were measured with a spectrophotometer (UV-3600, Japan) in the range of 200–800 nm. The transient photocurrent curve and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were recorded by a conventional three-electrode system on an electrochemical workstation (CHI6600E, CHENHUA Instrument Company). The electron spin resonance (ESR) of free radicals was measured with a JES-FA200 spectrometer under visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm), and the reactive oxygen species (ROS) were captured by 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) in methanol and deionized water. The details of photoelectrochemical (PEC) measurement were shown in SI.

The materials were utilized in photodegrading gaseous toluene under visible light irradiation and the structure of the reaction system was shown in Fig. S1. Gaseous toluene was prepared by injecting N2 into saturated toluene solution in ice bath at constant speed, and the concentration of gaseous toluene was controlled at 300 ± 20 ppm. Meanwhile, a stream of air was injected to dilute toluene gas in the followed mixed container. The air flows were controlled by air flow meters and the relative humidity remain constant at ∼63%.Then, the mixed gas flowed into a quartz reaction tube (10 cm in length). A 300 W Xenon lamp (light intensity was ∼1000 mW/cm2) with a 420 nm cut off filter was set above the quartz reaction tube with a distance of 10 cm. 0.1 g of the photocatalyst was uniformly tiled at the bottom of the quartz reaction tube. Then the residual gas was further purified by an exhaust device. The concentration of gaseous toluene was monitored via gas chromatography equipped with flame atomic detector (GC-FID) online. Prior to the reaction, the whole system was stabilized for 2.5 h in the dark to reach the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. A gas chromatography (GC, JN.SE-54, 30 m × 0.25 μm) was used to detect the concentration of toluene. The inlet gas was ∼220 ppm toluene. The total flow rate was kept at 156.3 mL/min. The injection temperature, detector temperature and the column temperature were 120 °C, 150 °C and 100 °C, respectively. All experiments were carried out at atmospheric pressure and room temperature, the removal efficiency (E) of toluene was calculated as follows:
(1)

where C was the concentration of resident toluene in the reactor and C0 was the initial concentration of toluene.

Optical properties play a significant part in the detection of NCDs which has fluorescent property. Fig. 1 a showed the fluorescence spectra of NCDs recorded at the range of excitation wavelengths from 350 nm to 510 nm with an increment of 20 nm. The fluorescence of NCDs will be affected by particle size, surface functional groups and the distribution of trap sites. As can be seen, the emission peak shifted from 490 nm to 570 nm as the excitation wave length increased from 350 nm to 510 nm. The emission intensity increased when the excitation wavelength increased from 350 nm to 430 nm since the intrinsic absorption range of NCDs is 430 nm and then decreased. Furthermore, NCDs showed an up-converted photoluminescence between 400 and 590 nm with the increased excitation wavelength at the range of 600–760 nm (Fig. 1b), indicating that the as-prepared NCDs could emit high-energetic light when irradiated by long-wave light and thus generate more e−-h+ pairs, which could facilitate the SPR effect of Ag and thus showed strong visible light response because of the localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) excitation [51].

The crystal structures of the as-prepared samples were evaluated by XRD patterns. As shown in Fig. 2 a, pure TiO2 was well matched with anatase phase TiO2 (JCPDS 21–1272) with six characteristic peaks at 25.2°, 37.7°, 48.0°, 53.9°, 54.9° and 62.5°, which were attributed to the (101), (004), (200), (105), (211) and (204) planes, respectively. For DAT, there are three peaks at 27.5°,45.5° and 64.0° which are corresponded with the Ag2O (JCPDS 76–2388) and (200) and (220) crystal planes of Ag (JCPDS 65–2871), revealing the incomplete reduction of Ag2O and further determined the co-existence of Ag and Ag2O. However, except for the other two peaks, the peak at 45.5° was not appeared on the XRD spectrum after the addition of NCDs. The doping of NCDs may affect the crystal formation of Ag2O, thus sheltering the characteristic peak. No significant diffraction peaks of NCDs were observed in DACT, which may probably because of the low doping content or the high dispersion degree of NCDs [52,53]. Furthermore, the characteristic peaks of TiO2 could be observed obviously in all samples, indicating that the introduction of Ag/Ag2O and/or NCDs would not destroy the structure of TiO2.

FTIR spectra were carried out to investigate the interaction between the atoms of photocatalysts. Fig. 2b exhibits the FTIR spectra of TiO2, DAT and DACT. The broad and strong peaks at around 500 cm−1, 1630 cm−1 and 3200–3600 cm−1 could associated with bending vibrations of O–H groups, physical adsorbed water with hydroxyl groups and Ti–O–Ti bridging stretching mode, respectively [38,54]. A weak peak at 1384 cm−1 was appeared due to the N–H deformation of NCDs in DACT [54]. Moreover, the introduction of NCDs introduces four weak peaks at 1166 cm−1, 1240 cm−1, 2854 cm−1 and 2925 cm−1, which could result from the vibrations of CO and C–H groups, showing that the NCDs was successful doped with TiO2 via the impregnation-calcination method [55]. It was also observed that the broad peaks at around 500 cm−1 showed a red-shift to higher wavenumber compared with bare TiO2, which could attribute to the combination of Ti–O–Ti and Ti–O–C vibrations [56] and further proved that the successful incorporation between NCDs and TiO2. The absorption peaks of Ag or Ag2O are not observed in DAT and DACT due to the low content.

To further investigate the morphology of the as-prepared composites, the TEM and HRTEM analysis have been carried out. As can be seen in Fig. 3 a, the section view of TiO2 shows the shape of hexagon, square and diamond, indicating TiO2 are consisted of truncated double pyramid nanoparticles. As shown in Fig. 3d, the HRTEM image displayed that there are two kinds of lattice fringes in the TiO2. The lattice fringe of 0.354 nm corresponding with the (101) planes of anatase TiO2, while another one with 0.235 nm was in agreement with the spacing of the (001) plane [57,58]. The morphology and particle size of TiO2 could be retained after the combination with Ag/Ag2O (Fig. 3b), which was in consistent with the XRD results, suggesting that the addition of Ag/Ag2O would not affect the formation of TiO2. In addition, constructing the heterojunction could facilitate the transport of photoinduced charge carriers. From Fig. 3e, despite the (101) facets of anatase TiO2, the new lattice fringe with the spacing of 0.270 nm could be indexed to the (111) plane of Ag2O while the other one of 0.204 nm could be indexed to the (200) plane of Ag [59,60]. Fig. 3c and f showed the morphologied of DACT composite. The NCDs nanoparticles are all attached to the surface of DAT and could act as charge transport medium, which could shorten the migration distance of the charge carriers and thus improving the photocatalytic activity. The new fringe with the spacing of 0.225 nm were found in the HRTEM image of DACT, corresponding to the NCDs (101) [61]. The TEM results demonstrated the successful fabrication of the DACT photocatalyst.

The Raman spectra was analyzed to study the phase compositions of different samples. As can be seen in Fig. S3, five characteristic peaks at around 140.6, 195.9, 393.8, 515.8 and 635.9 cm−1 could be correspond to 3 Eg modes, 2 B1g modes and 1 A1g mode of anatase TiO2 [67]. After the introduction of Ag/Ag2O and further combination with NCDs, the intensity was decreased, but there were still six peaks of the anatase phase in DAT and DACT spectra, indicating that silver and NCDs could not destroy the structure of TiO2. Moreover, anatase TiO2 was the dominant phase and there was no rutile phase TiO2.

UV–vis DRS of the as-prepared samples were carried out to verify the optical absorption properties. For bare TiO2 in Fig. 5 a, the absorbance edge was showed at 398 nm, which could be due to the electron transition from the anti-bonding orbital of O2 − to the lowest vacancy orbital of Ti4+ (O 2p→Ti 3d) [68]. However, Ag2O showed an intense absorbance in the range of 200–800 nm, indicating that Ag2O could effectively enhance the response to visible light. After TiO2 combining with Ag/Ag2O, the light absorbance edge showed a little red-shift and exhibited enhanced light intensity in the visible region, which could attribute to the SPR effect of Ag that could improve the absorbance of visible light. The absorption intensity of DACT was the highest owing to the SPR effect of Ag/Ag2O and the optical properties of NCDs. Hence, the introduction of Ag/Ag2O could improve the light absorption activity and the separation of charge carriers by forming p-n heterojunction, which was consistent with previous reports, while the doping of NCDs could reduce the demand for short wavelengths light and improve the charge transfer as a charge transfer medium. Such a synergetic effect resulted from the further addition of NCDs could account for the improvement of the photocatalytic property of the ternary DACT photocatalyst.

The Eg values of the as-prepared samples are displayed in Fig. 5b. The co-doped photocatalyst possessed lower band gap about 3.27 eV than bare TiO2 (3.37 eV) and DAT (3.35 eV), further suggesting that the existence of Ag/Ag2O and NCDs could synergetic promote the light absorption of TiO2. The valence band (VB) and CB positions of TiO2 and Ag2O were calculated by the following formulas [69,70]:

where X, Ee, EVB and ECB are the absolute electronegativity of the photocatalyst (XTiO2 = 5.81 eV, XAg2O = 5.30 eV), the energy of free electrons vs. Hydrogen (4.5 eV), the VB and CB potential, respectively. The VB and CB potentials of TiO2 and Ag2O were calculated and listed in Table 2 .

It is well known that the PL intensity is originated from the recombination of photoinduced e−-h+ pairs [71]. Low PL intensity indicates the low recombination rate and the long lifetime of photoinduced charge carriers, which could improve the photocatalytic performance of the photocatalyst. As shown in Fig. 6 , DAT exhibited lower PL intensity compared with TiO2, indicating that both the p-n heterojunction between TiO2 and Ag2O and the metallic Ag act as conductive bridge could improve the separation of photoinduced charge carriers and thus effectively suppress the recombination of photoinduced e−-h+ pairs [28]. Moreover, the introduction of NCDs could act as charge transfer bridge and thus facilitate the separation of e−-h+ pairs, leading to the best photocatalytic performance among these materials. The reason could be due to the formation of p-n heterojunction between TiO2 and Ag2O along with the enhanced visible-light response and separation of photoinduced e−-h+ pairs caused by Ag and NCDs, which could increase the chance of photoinduced charge carriers to participate in the photocatalytic reactions and thus enhancing the photocatalytic activity [72]. In short, according to the characterization above, DACT was successfully synthesized and has potential capacity for practical utilization.

The photocatalytic performance of TiO2, DAT and DACT were evaluated towards gaseous toluene under the irradiation of a 300 W xenon lamp as visible light source. The toluene removal experiments were conducted in the dark conditions at first for 120 min to reach the adsorption-desorption equilibrium, after which visible light was provided. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the concentration of gaseous toluene remained stable for all photocatalysts with the removal efficiency less than 2% which could attribute to the adsorption at the beginning. When irradiated with visible light, the toluene removal efficiency intensely increased, indicating the occurrence of photocatalytic process. The removal of toluene by TiO2 reached a balance after 50 min of visible-light irradiation with the removal efficiency of about 7.5%. However, the removal efficiency of gaseous toluene by DAT rose to 45.6% at first and then decreased to 23.3% in the following reaction. The possible reason that account for this phenomenon may be that the instability of Ag/Ag2O made the whole photocatalyst to be easily passivated during the photocatalytic reaction, resulting in the unsatisfactory removal efficiency [20]. And the little enhancement of photocatalytic performance could attributed to the addition of a little amount of Ag (1.5%) compared with previous researches [22,48], which could improve the stability of TiO2. The photocatalytic performance was significantly improved with the introduction of NCDs with the removal efficiency up to 49.1%, which increased by 25.1% compared with DAT. The further addition of NCDs could act as a charge medium to facilitate the visible-light response and the separation of photoinduced e−-h+ pairs due to the excellent optical properties especially the up-conversion fluorescence property.

The dosage of photocatalyst plays a significant role during the degradation process of gaseous toluene. With the increase of the dosage from 0.05 g to 0.1 g, the removal efficiency of gaseous toluene was increased from 9.6% to 46.9% as shown in Fig. 7b. However, the removal efficiency was almost unchanged when the dosage was further increased to 0.25 g. There are two reason that account for this phenomenon. On the one hand, the dosage of photocatalyst determined the contact area between toluene and photocatalyst. Thus, the higher photocatalyst dosage could provide more active sites for the removal reaction. On the other hand, excess dosage could block or overlapped of the active sites which makes the photocatalyst unable to make the full use of visible light due to the aggregation [73], resulting in the low photoelectron-hole yield, thus affecting the photocatalytic performance.

The inlet concentration of gaseous toluene also affects the photocatalytic performance. As depicted in Fig. 7c, the removal efficiency of gaseous toluene reached the highest (44.9%) at the inlet concentration of 150 ppm. The increasing of the inlet concentration of gaseous toluene is unfavorable to the improvement of the removal efficiency, which was in accordance with the result of previous research [74]. When irradiated with visible light, the photocatalyst could be excited and produce a certain amount of charge carriers, which could further generate reactive species such as ⎕OH, ⎕O2 − and photoinduced h+. The number of reactive species generated on the photocatalyst surface is constant while the power of light and the dosage of photocatalyst are constant. As a consequence, with the increase of the inlet concentration, the reactive species generated on the photocatalyst was not enough to trigger the reaction, thus resulting in the low degradation efficiency.

The H2O molecules adsorbed on the surface of photocatalyst are capable to form ⎕OH which is one of the reactive species involved in photocatalytic process. Thus, the relative humidity also affects the photocatalytic performance. The optimal range of relative humidity should be determined according to the specific experimental conditions. The influence of relative humidity on the removal of gaseous toluene was shown in Fig. 7d. Similar to the discussion above, the removal efficiency was rose at first and then decreased to reach equilibrium due to the unstable character of Ag2O. The removal efficiency of gaseous toluene has a maximum of 49.1% with the relative humidity of 37 ± 5%. The relative humidity has two effects on photocatalytic degradation process. The photoinduced h+ on the VB of DACT would react with the H2O molecules existed in the air which adsorbed on the surface of photocatalyst and then generate ⎕OH to oxidize the gaseous toluene [75]. However, the H2O molecules could adsorb on the surface of TiO2 by binding with –OH, which could hinder the transfer of the reactive species on the surface or the boundary layer of the photocatalyst [76]. On the other hand, H2O molecules could compete with gaseous toluene for the adsorption and photocatalytic sites, thus restrain the photocatalytic process [77].

Moreover, the velocity of gaseous toluene was also investigated to verify the influence of different velocity. Increasing the velocity could reducing the residence time while increasing the convection mass transfer rate [78,79]. As can be seen in Fig. S4, there was an optimal velocity, 156.3 mL/min, during the photocatalytic degradation of gaseous toluene. The removal efficiency increased from 22.3% to 36.1 as the velocity of gaseous toluene rose from 126.8 mL/min to 156.3 mL/min. However, further increase of the velocity of gaseous toluene reduced the removal efficiency. According to previous research, the velocity of gaseous pollutant has a low airflow regimes, in which the mass transfer between the photocatalyst and the pollutant was the rate-determining step (RDS) [79,80]. Increasing the velocity of gaseous toluene could enhance the mass transfer, resulting in high removal efficiency. Nevertheless, the excessive flow rate could lead to insufficient contact time between gaseous toluene and photocatalyst, which makes the photocatalytic reaction unable to proceed successfully, thus reducing the removal efficiency.

The stability of the photocatalyst is a key factor in its practical utilization. As shown in Fig. 8 a and Fig. S5, the XRD and FTIR patterns of the used samples had almost no obvious difference compared with the former one, which clearly prove the structure stability of the DACT under light irradiation. Furthermore, long-time test was also carried out to test the stability of the DACT for degrading gaseous toluene (Fig. 8b). The efficiency of toluene removal was steady during 400 min of photocatalysis without obvious decline, indicating the good photostability of the DACT.

In order to verify the mechanism of the enhancement of toluene removal, the ESR technique was further carried out to detect the generation of active radicals in the DACT composite samples under visible light irradiation. DMPO was used as the spin trapping reagent in methanol (for DMPO-·O2 −) and H2O (for DMPO-·OH) solution. As shown in Fig. 9 a, no obvious signal peaks in the and DMPO-·O2 − were found at dark. However, after exposed to visible light for 10 min, six characteristic peaks of DMPO-·O2 − were observed, suggesting ·O2 − was generated during photocatalytic process. Similarly, four characteristic peaks with an intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1 were observed in the ESR spectrum (Fig. 9b), whose intensity is significantly higher than that of DMPO-·O2 −. The results indicated that both ·OH and ·O2 − could be generated under visible-light irradiation and participate in the photocatalytic process.

Based on the band gap energy and the result of ESR measurement, a possible photocatalytic mechanism was proposed and displayed in Fig. 10 . When visible light irradiated on DACT, both TiO2 and Ag2O could be excited directly and indirectly because of the up-conversion feature of NCDs which could emit high-energetic light (λ < 420 nm) when irradiated by long-wave light (λ > 420 nm) to generate more e−-h+ pairs, thus improving the photocatalytic performance. The e− on the VB of TiO2 and Ag2O were produced and transferred to the CB, leaving h+ in the VB. According to the traditional type-Ⅰ heterojunction mechanism (electrons and holes could easily migrated to lower band position) [81], the photoinduced e− on the CB of TiO2 will transfer to the CB of Ag2O and the photoinduced h+ in the VB of TiO2 will also transfer to the VB of Ag2O, resulting in the photoinduced e− and h+ accumulation on the Ag2O. Meantime, NCDs could act as the charge transfer bridge between TiO2 and Ag2O to trap a portion of e− excited from TiO2 and transfer to the Ag2O owing to excellent conductivity. Then, Ag0 on Ag2O surface could improve the generation of the photoinduced charge carriers, separation efficiency of photoinduced e−-h+ pairs, and further enhance photocatalytic performance due to the localized surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect. As a consequence, photoinduced e− were captured and accumulated on the surface of Ag0, which were separated from photoinduced h+ and hindered the recombination. On the other hand, in accordance with previous reports, the sequence of electron transfer could judge by work function [82]. The work function of Ag0 is about 4.2 eV [83]. As NCDs was graphitic-like and also has some oxygen-rich groups, it could be considered as graphene oxide (GO) with a work function of 4.7–5.2 eV [83]. Thus, the electrons will partially transfer from Ag0 to NCDs since electrons tend to move to materials with higher work function. Based on the discussion above, the e− on the NCDs and Ag2O would react with O2 to yield ·O2 −. According to the ESR result, ·OH was generated during the photocatalysis. However, the accumulated h+ on the VB of Ag2O are reluctant to trigger –OH or H2O to generate ·OH radicals because the VB potential of Ag2O (1.53 V vs NHE) is negative than the standard redox potential of both H2O/·OH (2.68 V vs NHE) or –OH/·OH (1.99 V vs NHE). Therefore, the transferred photo-induced electrons first combined with O2 to form ·O2 −, and ·O2 − further reacted with e− to generate ·OH (Eqs. (4)–(6)) which oxidizes the absorbed toluene. Moreover, although h+ on the VB of Ag2O cannot directly oxidize toluene, it also has a strong oxidation property to oxidize toluene to small molecule by redox reaction. All the above active species (·OH, ·O2 − and h+) could oxidize toluene and finally mineralize it into CO2, H2O and other nontoxic small molecules by photodegradation reaction. Based on the discussion above, the combination of the SPR effect of Ag and the up-conversion property of NCDs contributed to the improvement of photocatalytic performance.

A novel DACT photocatalyst was fabricated for the first time via facile chemical precipitation-reduction method. The photocatalytic degradation experiments were carried out with high concentration gaseous toluene as target pollutant under visible light. The DACT photocatalyst delivered a stable structure and showed the highest toluene removal efficiency of 49.1% compared with TiO2 (7.5%) and DAT (23.3%). The photocatalytic degradation on the main effect factors showed that the photocatalytic process could be further enhanced by optimize the reaction conditions. The excellent photocatalytic performance could attribute to the synergetic effect of the up-conversion effect and electrical conductivity of NCDs and the SPR effect of Ag which could promote the stability of p-n heterojunction. Furthermore, theoretical calculation revealed that the main active species involved in the reaction were ·O2 −, ·OH and h+. The results provide a novel insight into facile fabrication of photocatalyst and introduce DACT photocatalyst as an efficient visible-light-responsive photocatalyst for the treatment of VOCs in practical applications.
Xiuling Xue: Supervision, and Reviewing. Xiaoyi Chen: Experiments, Data curation, and Writing. Xiuwen Gong: Editing.
